coredata.dc:title: Synergy of zero-dimensional carbon dots decoration on the one-dimensional architecture of Ag-doped V2O5 for supercapacitor and overall water-splitting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Irshad, Amna
Zulfiqar, Sonia
A.Alothman, Zeid
Shakir, Imran
Farooq Warsi, Muhammad
Cochran, Eric W.
coredata.dc:description: The production of renewable energy sources and energy storage devices is crucial in addressing current global energy challenges. Hydrogen energy is a clean form of energy that can be produced without any harmful by-products. For this purpose, nanorods of vanadium oxide (V2O5) and silver-doped vanadium oxide (Ag/V2O5) were synthesized by hydrothermal route. The carbon dots decorated silver doped vanadium oxide (Ag/V2O5@C) was fabricated using an ultrasonication approach. Various physio-chemical techniques were used to characterize the fabricated samples. The synthesized materials were employed as electrodes and electrocatalysts for supercapacitor and water-splitting applications. Cyclic voltammetry and cyclic charge–discharge experiments were performed, and results showed that Ag/V2O5@C exhibited 936 Fg−1 specific capacitance at 5 mVs−1 and 977 s discharge time. The charge transfer resistance was calculated via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Ag/V2O5@C showed a lower charge transfer resistance than other prepared materials. At 10 mAcm−2, Ag/V2O5@C exhibited lower overpotential of 126 mV and 388 mV for hydrogen evolution (HER) and oxygen evolution reactions (OER) respectively. The lower tafel slope of 81 mV dec−1 and 71 mV dec−1 was attributed to the Ag/V2O5@C for HER and OER respectively. Ag/V2O5@C showed higher reaction kinetics due to the fast rate of charge transfer, low resistance, high conductivity, and greater active sites provided by the carbon dots for electrocatalytic reaction. So, Ag/V2O5@C can be employed as an effective electrocatalyst and electrode material for electrochemical applications.

head.ce:title: Synergy of zero-dimensional carbon dots decoration on the one-dimensional architecture of Ag-doped V2O5 for supercapacitor and overall water-splitting applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The production of renewable energy sources and energy storage devices is crucial in addressing current global energy challenges. Hydrogen energy is a clean form of energy that can be produced without any harmful by-products. For this purpose, nanorods of vanadium oxide (V2O5) and silver-doped vanadium oxide (Ag/V2O5) were synthesized by hydrothermal route. The carbon dots decorated silver doped vanadium oxide (Ag/V2O5@C) was fabricated using an ultrasonication approach. Various physio-chemical techniques were used to characterize the fabricated samples. The synthesized materials were employed as electrodes and electrocatalysts for supercapacitor and water-splitting applications. Cyclic voltammetry and cyclic charge–discharge experiments were performed, and results showed that Ag/V2O5@C exhibited 936 Fg−1 specific capacitance at 5 mVs−1 and 977 s discharge time. The charge transfer resistance was calculated via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Ag/V2O5@C showed a lower charge transfer resistance than other prepared materials. At 10 mAcm−2, Ag/V2O5@C exhibited lower overpotential of 126 mV and 388 mV for hydrogen evolution (HER) and oxygen evolution reactions (OER) respectively. The lower tafel slope of 81 mV dec−1 and 71 mV dec−1 was attributed to the Ag/V2O5@C for HER and OER respectively. Ag/V2O5@C showed higher reaction kinetics due to the fast rate of charge transfer, low resistance, high conductivity, and greater active sites provided by the carbon dots for electrocatalytic reaction. So, Ag/V2O5@C can be employed as an effective electrocatalyst and electrode material for electrochemical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Depletion of fossil fuels due to their large consumption by the increasing population is a major problem at present. Energy demand becomes high day by day. The conventional sources of energy production cannot satisfy the needs of the world’s population. So, many developments have been introduced to produce, conserve, and store energy [1]. Li-ion batteries, lead acid batteries, fuel cells, and supercapacitors are electrochemical devices that can produce and store energy [2–7]. Among these energy storage devices, supercapacitors have attained great attention due to their environment-friendly nature, long life cycle, excellent efficiency, light-weight, and short time required for charging. Supercapacitors have a high power and low energy density than batteries [8].

The use of fossil fuels produces large amounts of harmful gases such as carbon dioxide, sulfur oxides, and nitrogen oxides. A lot of research has been performed to figure out the clean and renewable sources of energy. Hydrogen being the most abundant element on earth can be employed to produce energy without the formation of hazardous by-products [9,10]. As compared to other techniques, water splitting involves reactions that are reversed to hydrogen combustion and is the most clean and efficient approach for the production of the hydrogen [11]. The water-splitting process completes with no emission of carbon therefore it is considered a promising strategy to produce clean energy [12–14]. Electrochemical water splitting was first observed in 1789 and it typically takes place in an electrolyte. Oxidation and reduction half-reactions take place at the cathode and anode respectively [10]. An electrolyte is employed for the transportation of ions between electrodes. The overall chemical reaction is H2O → H2 + 1/2 O2.

In electrochemical water splitting, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) takes place at the cathode and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) occurs at the anode of the electrochemical cell [15,16]. Platinum showed better performance for hydrogen evolution reaction and RuO2 and IrO2 exhibited great capability for oxygen evolution reaction, but they cannot be employed for large-scale usage because of their low stability, high price, rarity, and limited shelf life [17–20]. So, a lot of work has been performed for the synthesis of cheap and stable electrode material for supercapacitor and water-splitting applications.

Various transition metal compounds were employed for electrochemical applications such as MoO3 [21], CuO [22], MoS2 [23,24], IrO2 [25], ZnO [26,27], WO3 [28], Ni(OH)2 [29], MnO2 [21,30], Co(OH)2 [31], ZnS [32], CoS [33], Fe(OH)3 [34], Co3O4 [35], and NiO [36,37]. Babakhani et al. reported the synthesis of Mn–Co oxide/PEDOT for electrochemical capacitors and showed 310 Fg−1 capacitance at 100 mVs−1 scan rate [38]. You et al. reported the fabrication of CFF@V2O5 for supercapacitor applications and the prepared composite showed 492Fg−1 specific capacitance and 0.928mWh cm−3 energy density [39]. Xiong et al. synthesized Ni3S2/MnO2 for the water splitting measurements which showed 102 mV and 260 mV overpotential at the current density of 10 mAcm−2 for HER and OER respectively in alkaline media [40].

Vanadium oxide is a promising material for electrochemical applications among transition metal oxides due to its multiple valences, cost effectiveness, easy availability, and high energy capacity [41–46]. Vanadium based materials attain great attention due to their excellent electrochemical activity and can behave as potential catalysts for HER and OER [43]. Vanadium oxide is rich in redox levels due to the existence of vanadium in the material as V5+. But it has low capability for the transmission of electrons, low conductance, high overpotential, and low production of oxygen [19,43]. These reasons limit the electrochemical application of vanadium oxide. So, doping of V2O5 with another metal and the formation of its composite with conducting materials can be employed. Graphene, reduced graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon fiber, activated carbon, and carbon dots can be used for composite formation [47–51].

Carbon dots have gained much attention in variety of fields such as sensors [52], solar cells [53], and light-emitting diodes [54], due to their small size, greater surface area, uniform shape, and efficient adaptive surface chemistry. Recently carbon dots were also employed as electrode material for supercapacitors because they improve the transportation of electrons, and movement of ions, and enhance the contact area between the electrolytes and electrodes used during electrochemical and electrocatalytic reactions [55–58].

Herein, we have synthesized vanadium oxide (V2O5) and silver-doped vanadium oxide (Ag/V2O5) via the hydrothermal route. The nanocomposite of silver-doped vanadium oxide with carbon dots was prepared using an ultrasonication approach (Ag/V2O5@C). The fabricated materials were characterized via X-ray diffraction analysis, field emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy and energy-dispersive X-ray analysis. The prepared samples were further employed for the supercapacitor and water-splitting applications.

The chemicals required for the synthesis of vanadium oxide, silver doped vanadium oxide, and carbon dots decorated silver doped vanadium oxide were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), nitric acid (HNO3), silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid (C6H8O7), urea (CH4N2O), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of V2O5 nanorods and silver doped V2O5
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of V2O5 nanorods was done via a hydrothermal route [59]. For the fabrication of V2O5 nanorods, 5 mM ammonium metavanadate was prepared in 50 mL of distilled water. The stirring of the solution was carried out until a homogenous solution was formed. Then to adjust the pH value to 3–4, nitric acid was slowly poured into the ammonium metavanadate solution. The stirring of the mixture was done for about half an hour. Then the mixture was transferred into Teflon- lined autoclave. The autoclave was kept in the oven for 24 h at 180 °C. After completion of the reaction, the precipitates were cooled at room temperature and then washed with distilled water to remove any impurities. The collected precipitates were dried at 60 °C in a laboratory oven overnight. To synthesize silver-doped V2O5, a stoichiometric amount of silver nitrate was added in addition to the ammonium metavanadate in water followed by the same procedure described above. The prepared samples were then used for characterization and application studies.

For the synthesis of carbon dots, an easy, affordable, and one-step microwave-assisted method was used [60]. Citric acid and urea were taken in stoichiometric amounts (1:1) and added to a beaker having 10 mL of distilled water. The resultant transparent solution was placed in a microwave oven for 5 min. The solution changed to a dark brown solid at the end of the reaction. The solid material was then kept in the vacuum oven at 60 °C for about one hour to remove the residues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silver doped V2O5@Carbon dots nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrication of the nanocomposite of silver-doped V2O5 with carbon dots, an ultrasonication approach was followed. 80 mg of silver-doped V2O5 and 20 mg of carbon dots were added to the 100 mL distilled water. The ultrasonication was carried out for about 2 h. Then the homogenized solution was dried in an oven at about 60 °C. The dried material was ground into a fine powder and used for characterization and application studies. Fig. 1 depicts the synthesis of carbon dots and their silver doped V2O5 nanocomposite formation.

Structural analysis of the as-prepared samples was carried out via Philips X’Pert, PRO 3040/60 X-ray diffractometer. The surface morphology of the prepared materials was examined using ZEISS LEO SUPRA 55 field emission scanning electron microscope. The elemental composition of the fabricated samples was investigated using JEOL JCM-6000Plus SEM. TEM images were recorded using JEOL JSM 2100 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The electrochemical studies such as cyclic voltammetry, cyclic charge–discharge, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopic analysis and water splitting measurements such as linear sweep voltammetry were performed via GAMRY interface/5000E potentiostat.

To perform electrochemical experiments for supercapacitor and water splitting study, first of all, the working electrode was prepared. 0.3 mg of the fabricated material was taken and ethanol was added to it. After proper mixing, 50 μL of Nafion binder was added to form a thick paste. The paste was loaded onto the nickel foam. The electrode was dried at room temperature and was employed as the working electrode [61]. The three-electrode system was used to perform the electrochemical measurements. Nickel foam-supported V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C were used as working electrodes. Platinum was used as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. The electrolyte employed was 2 M potassium hydroxide. To study the super-capacitive behavior of the fabricated materials, cyclic voltammetry was conducted at different scan rates (5–100 mVs−1) and cyclic charge–discharge experiment was performed at different current densities. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis was employed to study the resistance faced by the electrodes in the electrolyte during electrochemical measurements. For water splitting measurements, linear sweep voltammetry was performed at 5 mVs−1 for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). The overpotential, onset potential, and Tafel slope were calculated with the help of LSV polarization curves, and interpretation of the results was performed to figure out the best electrocatalyst among the fabricated materials.

The structure of the fabricated samples was investigated via X-ray diffraction analysis. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C between 2 theta values of 10 −70°. XRD pattern of V2O5 confirmed its orthorhombic crystal system and Pmmn space group. V2O5 showed hkl planes (002), (200), (221), (201), (240), (301), (001), (111), (440), (211), (401), (510), (112), (046), (021), (412), (321), and (003) at the 2 theta value of 14.97°,15.83°, 18.45°, 25.39°, 27.54°, 30.72°, 32.15°, 33.88°, 35.68°, 36.54°, 37.46°, 46.86°, 49.52°, 50.54°, 55.44°, 59.63°, 61.06°, and 63.93° respectively (JCPDS # 01-077-2418 & 00-045-1074). There was no extra peak observed in the XRD of V2O5 which confirms its formation without any impurity and its highly crystalline nature. The XRD patterns of silver-doped V2O5 and its nanocomposite with carbon dots showed no extra peaks but slight changes in the intensity of the XRD peaks. The crystallite size and dislocation density were enumerated using the following Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)

K λ
β cos θ

Where, D = Crystallite size, K = Shape factor, λ = Radiation wavelength, β = Full width at half maxima, and θ = Bragg’s angle.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the decrease in the crystallite size from V2O5 to Ag/V2O5@C. The crystallite size of silver doped vanadium oxide is less than that of bare vanadium oxide. The decrease in the crystallite size is due to the insertion of the silver ions into the lattice of vanadium oxide which causes distortion and decrease in the nucleation sites and growth of the bare V2O5 [62]. The smaller crystallite size provides enough surface area for the catalytic reactions. Fig. 3 (b) exhibits the plot of dislocation density for the prepared samples.

The surface morphology of the fabricated materials was examined via field emission scanning electron microscopy. FESEM analysis was carried out at different magnifications to figure out the morphology of the prepared samples. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) shows the FESEM micrographs for V2O5 and Ag/V2O5. The FESEM results of both samples revealed the formation of nanorods. Fig. 4 (c) further confirmed the formation of carbon dots via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Ag/V2O5@C nanocomposite formation was verified using FESEM analysis, revealing the presence of both carbon and nanorods as expected (Fig. 4 (d)). Fig. 5 shows the diameter distribution histogram of (a) V2O5 and (b) Ag/V2O5. V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 show average diameters of 69.58 nm and 70.59 nm respectively. The histogram plot was fitted via the Gaussian model and the value of Adj R-square was computed which was 0.745 and 0.882 for V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 respectively. The elemental analysis of the prepared materials was done via energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. Fig. 6 shows the EDX profiles for (a) V2O5, (b) Ag/V2O5, and (c) Ag/V2O5@C respectively. In the EDX profile of V2O5, the peaks of vanadium and oxygen are present. The EDX spectra of silver-doped vanadium oxide showed the peak of silver along the peaks of vanadium and oxygen. The peaks for carbon can also be visualized along the peaks of silver, vanadium, and oxygen in the EDX profile of Ag/V2O5@C. There are no extra peaks observed in all the samples that confirm the synthesis of pure vanadium oxide, silver-doped vanadium oxide, and nanocomposite of carbon dots with silver-doped vanadium oxide.
For the supercapacitor study, experiments such as cyclic voltammetry, cyclic charge–discharge, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopic analysis were performed using three electrode system.

The cyclic voltammetry was performed at the scan rate of 5–100 mVsec−1 in the potential range of 0.2–0.5 V. Fig. 7 shows the cyclic voltammogram of (a) V2O5, (b) Ag/V2O5, and (c) Ag/V2O5@C. CV plots of as-prepared materials showed prominent redox peaks in the anodic and cathodic areas. The redox peaks are due to the faradic reactions between an electrolyte and the surface of the electrode [63].
(3) V2O5 + OH− ↔ V2O5OH + e −
(4) V2O5 + K+ + e− ↔ V2O5 − K+

The specific capacitance can be computed via the following Eq. (5):
(5)
Csp
=
Current
Density ( A / g )
2 × Δ V × S c a n
r a t e

In equation (5), the current density is the integrated area of the cyclic voltammogram loop,
Δ V
denotes the potential window in which the reduction and oxidation peaks appear. The current density and integrated CV area are larger for the Ag/V2O5@C and smaller for the pristine V2O5. Ag/V2O5 has a current density and integrated CV area in between the V2O5 and Ag/V2O5@C. It implies that the Ag/V2O5@C has greater charge storage ability and hence higher capacitance than the pristine V2O5 and Ag/V2O5.

To figure out the diffusion and capacitance contribution during the charge storage process, Dunn’s method was followed [64]. These two processes are explained with the help of Eqs. (6) and (7).

In above equations,

shows the diffusion-controlled process at specific scan rate. Fig. 7 (d) shows the contribution ratio of Ag/V2O5@C at different scan rates. At 100 mVs−1, Ag/V2O5@C shows 84 % capacitive contribution and 16 % diffusion contribution while At 5 mVs−1, Ag/V2O5@C shows 54 % capacitive contribution and 46 % diffusion contribution. The capacitance contribution (%) is increasing with the increasing scan rate because of the fast adsorption of electrolyte ions and diffusion contribution decreases due to the less time available for diffusion of electrolyte ions on the electrode surface.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the specific capacitance of 575, 676, and 936 Fg−1 at 5 mVs−1 for the V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C respectively. There is no distortion observed in the CV loops of the as-prepared electrodes which showed the stability of the redox process. The position of the oxidation and reduction peaks changes as the scan rate changes from 5 −100 mVs−1. The increase in the scan rate is responsible for less interaction between the electrode and electrolyte ions. So, the capacitance decreases with the increasing scan rate as shown in Fig. 8 (b). The stability of the as prepared Ag/V2O5@C nanocomposite was checked through the cyclic voltammetry for 3000 cycles. Fig. 8 (c) shows the curves at first cycle and after 3000 cycles at the scan rate of 5 mVs−1. The specific capacitance was calculated after every 1000 cycles and is shown in Fig. 8 (d). Ag/V2O5@C shows specific capacitance of 936 Fg−1 at first cycle and 856 Fg−1 after 3000 cycles. There decrease in capacitance is only 9 % even after 3000 cycles which means that the Ag/V2O5@C is a stable electrode material for electrochemical measurements.

The charge storage mechanism on the electrolyte and electrode interface of V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C was estimated using the power law [65]. The anodic and cathodic currents depend on the scan rate and can be described via the following Equation (8):
(8)
log
i
= b log
v
+ log
( a )

In Eq. (8), i denote the current,
v
is the scan rate and b represents the kinetic constant. The value of b describes the type of charge storage mechanism. If value of b = 0.5, then the mechanism is diffusion limited, and if b = 1 then the electrode follows the capacitive method of charge storage. Fig. 9 presents the plot of log scan rate vs log of anodic and cathodic current of (a) V2O5, (b) Ag/V2O5, and (c) Ag/V2O5@C. The results show that value of b is between the 0.5 and 1 so the prepared materials show both diffusion-limited and capacitive mechanisms for charge storage. To investigate the diffusion-limited process happening in the V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C electrodes, the plot between square root of the scan rate and anodic current was drawn as presented in Fig. 9 (d). The value of diffusion coefficient (D) was calculated after linear fitting. V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C were assigned the D value of 0.004, 0.005, and 0.011 respectively. As Ag/V2O5@C has a greater value of D than other two electrode materials so it showed better conductivity, diffusion process, and in turn highest capacitance among all.

Cyclic charge–discharge curves were observed at current densities of 1, 1.2, 1.4, and 1.6 Ag−1. Fig. 10 depicts the discharge curves of (a) V2O5, (b) Ag/V2O5, and (c) Ag/V2O5@C. The pseudocapacitive nature of the fabricated electrodes was assured via CCD curves. The CCD results support the findings of CV showing that electrodes presented the faradic mechanism of charge storage. Ag/V2O5@C showed a higher discharge time than pristine V2O5 and Ag/V2O5. 676 s, 904 s, and 977 s discharge time was shown by the V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C respectively at the current density of 1 Ag−1. The capacitance was also calculated using discharge time from cyclic charge–discharge measurements. The following equation (9) was employed to find out the capacitance of the as-prepared samples [61].
(9)
Csp
=
I × Δ t
3.6 × m

V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C showed capacitance of 625 mAhg−1, 837 mAhg−1, and 903 mAhg−1 respectively at 1 Ag−1 current density. Ag/V2O5@C showed higher specific capacitance than other prepared electrodes because of the existence of active sites, greater electrical conductivity, and greater surface area that the carbon dots provided to the silver-doped V2O5 for efficient charge transfer during the electrochemical reaction. Fig. 10 (d) exhibits the capacitance of all samples to current densities and it can be observed that specific capacitance is decreasing with the increasing value of current density. Electrolyte ions cannot penetrate deeply into a functioning electrode because of the extra charges that build up on an electrode's surface at high current densities and results in less interaction between electrode and electrolyte hence the capacitance becomes less at higher current densities. Table 1 shows the parameters calculated at 5 mVs−1 scan rate and 1 Ag−1 current density during electrochemical measurements.

To check the electrochemical activity of the prepared electrodes, an EIS test was conducted between 0.1 Hz and 100 kHz frequency range in 2 M KOH solution. EIS was performed to analyze the resistance and transportation of electrons and ions during electrochemical measurements. Fig. 11 shows the Nyquist plots of V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C. In the Nyquist plot, Z′ denotes the real resistance, and Z′′ represents the imaginary resistance. The fitting of the EIS data was done using the EC lab software. Table 2 shows the resistances obtained from the EIS plots of the as prepared samples. R1 is the solution resistance and its value for V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C is 1.03, 0.39, and 0.21 Ohm respectively. The carbon dots-based nanocomposite experiences less solution resistance during electrochemical measurements than other prepared samples. R2 represents the charge transfer resistance and its value for V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C is 1.15, 0.65, and 0.35 Ohm respectively. Ag/V2O5@C showed less charge transfer resistance than the pristine V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 that is why Ag/V2O5@C shows a greater rate for transfer of charges and exhibits the pseudocapacitive behavior.

The electrocatalytic performance of the fabricated catalysts was investigated for the production of hydrogen and oxygen via linear sweep voltammetry. KOH was used as an electrolyte in a three-electrode system.

The catalytic activity of the V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C was investigated via linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements in 2 M KOH solution. IR corrected LSV plot for HER activity of as-fabricated electrodes is shown in Fig. 12 . V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C showed onset potential at 0.16 V, 0.11 V, and 0.08 V respectively. The value of overpotential for V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C at 10 mAcm−2 is 189 mV, 162 mV, and 126 mV respectively. Ag/V2O5@C exhibited a low value of overpotential than other catalysts. To figure out the kinetics of HER activity Tafel slope was determined and the graph was plotted between log J and overpotential values. The Tafel slope for V2O5, Ag/V2O5 and Ag/V2O5@C was fitted to be 212, 107, and 81 mV dec−1 respectively. The lower value of Tafel slope of Ag/V2O5@C shows excellent reaction kinetics during HER activity and good efficiency for the transfer of electrons during the catalytic reaction. Tafel slope reveals the mechanism for hydrogen production. The following expressions show the mechanism of H2 production during HER.

Volmer Reaction:
(10) M* + H2O + e− ↔ H*+ OH−(Discharge step)

Heyrovsky reaction:
(11) H* + H2O + e− ↔ H2↑ + OH− (Desorption step)

Tafel reaction:
(12) 2H* ↔ H2 ↑ (Recombination step)

M* denotes the active sites of the electrocatalyst. In the 1st step, the water molecule gets adsorbed on the active sites of the catalyst to form activated hydrogen radicals. This is the rate-determining step. The activated hydrogen radicals will either absorb another molecule of water during Heyrovsky reaction or the hydrogen radical converts to the H2 gas during the Tafel reaction. Overall, two H2O molecules are involved in the HER mechanism and one molecule of hydrogen gas is formed. The lower overpotential and Tafel slope of Ag/V2O5@C as compared to other fabricated electrocatalysts depicts the marvelous electrocatalytic performance. The greater electrocatalytic performance of nanocomposite is due to the enhanced conductivity and surface area provided by the carbon dots.

The catalytic activity of the V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C was investigated via linear sweep voltammetry measurements in 2 M KOH solution at 5 mVs−1. IR corrected LSV plot for OER activity of the fabricated electrodes is shown in Fig. 13 . V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C showed onset potential at 1.59 V, 1.57 V, and 1.55 V respectively. The value of overpotential at 10 mAcm−2 for V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C is 442 mV, 398 mV, and 388 mV respectively. Ag/V2O5@C exhibited a low value of overpotential than other catalysts. To figure out the kinetics of OER activity, Tafel slope was determined and the graph was plotted between log J and overpotential values. The Tafel slope for V2O5, Ag/V2O5 and Ag/V2O5@C was fitted to be 135, 125, and 71 mV dec−1 respectively. A small Tafel slope and lower value of overpotential for Ag/V2O5@C show the high reaction kinetics and efficient OER activity for oxygen production. The rate-limiting step for OER can be determined using the value of the Tafel slope. Oxygen evolution reaction occurs through multiple steps in the alkaline solution as presented in the following expressions.

The overall mechanism of OER reactions is:

The OER mechanism involves the adsorption of hydroxyl groups on the active sites of the catalyst. There are 4 steps involved in the oxygen evolution reaction. Firstly, OH– converts to OH* on the material active sites. M* shows the active sites of the material. Secondly, OH* was converted to the reactive radicals of oxygen with the elimination of H2O molecules and e−. In the 3rd step, OH– reacts with the M − O* and M − OOH* with the release of e−. The 4th step involves the reaction between M − OOH* and OH– to form an O2 molecule, e−, H2O molecule, and catalyst active sites. Overall the generation of O2 molecules involves four hydroxyl ions with the release of two H2O molecules and electron [66]. Fig. 14 represents the reactions occurring in the HER and OER measurements of Ag/V2O5@C. Ag/V2O5@C showed outstanding performance than V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 for HER and OER due to the smooth mass diffusion, fast rate of charge transfer, low resistance, high conductivity, and greater active sites for electrocatalytic reaction. Table 3 exhibits the parameters calculated from water splitting experiment in 2 M KOH at 5 mVs−1 scan rates.

V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 nanorods were prepared via hydrothermal route. Ag/V2O5@C was prepared by the ultrasonication of carbon dots and Ag/V2O5. The fabricated materials were employed as electrode materials for supercapacitor study and as electrocatalysts for water-splitting measurements. Structural confirmation was done by X-ray diffraction analysis. 2 M potassium hydroxide was used as an electrolyte. EIS analysis was performed between 0.1 Hz and 100 kHz to find out the electrical resistance of the materials. V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C showed specific capacitance of 575, 676, and 936 Fg−1 at 5 mVs−1. The discharge time was calculated by CCD measurement and V2O5, Ag/V2O5, and Ag/V2O5@C showed 676 s, 904 s, and 977 s discharge time respectively. Ag/V2O5@C presented specific capacitance of 903 mAhg−1 at 1 Ag−1 current density. Ag/V2O5@C showed lower overpotential (126 mV) and Tafel slope (81 mVdec−1) than V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 during the HER experiment. Ag/V2O5@C showed lower overpotential (388 mV) and Tafel slope (71 mVdec−1) than V2O5 and Ag/V2O5 during the OER experiment. The small value of the Tafel slope and overpotential for Ag/V2O5@C exhibit high reaction kinetics. Ag/V2O5@C shows efficient OER and HER activity for oxygen and hydrogen production respectively. The greater activity of Ag/V2O5@C in electrochemical experiments is because of the existence of active sites, greater electrical conductivity and higher surface area that carbon dots provide to the silver-doped V2O5 for efficient charge transfer during the electrochemical reaction. Therefore, Ag/V2O5@C can be utilized as an excellent electrode material for supercapacitors and electrocatalyst for energy production.

Amna Irshad: Methodology. Sonia Zulfiqar: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zeid A.Alothman: Writing – review & editing. Imran Shakir: Formal analysis. Muhammad Farooq Warsi: Supervision. Eric W. Cochran: Resources, Formal analysis.
